title: Corner Majorana states in semi-Dirac
authors: M. García Olmos, Y. Baba, R. A. Molina, M. Amado
abstract: Proximity-induced superconductivity in low-dimensional systems offers a powerful pathway to engineer topological superconducting phases in, otherwise, non-superconducting systems. These exotic phases are of fundamental and technological interest due to the presence of robust zero-energy modes, the Majorana bound states. In this work, we propose a theoretical framework to realize Majorana bound states from the edge states of a two-dimensional semi-Dirac system. This anisotropic system, under specific conditions, can host non-chiral edge states that propagate only along particular edges, effectively forming separated one-dimensional channels. We show that the interplay between Rashba spin-orbit coupling and a Zeeman field on this setup provides the right conditions to get an effective p-wave pairing between the edge states by proximity with a s-wave superconductor. In finite geometries, each edge can independently undergo a topological phase transition into a one-dimensional topological superconductor and give rise to four zero-energy modes localized at the strip corners. At low energies, the edge states subspace admits a description in terms of coupled Kitaev chains, providing a clear picture of the origin, robustness, and tunability of the corner Majorana modes. Our results establish semi-Dirac materials as a natural platform for realizing Majorana modes in two dimensions without relying on engineered nanostructures, vortices, or crystalline higher-order topology.
summary: This paper proposes a new theoretical platform for realizing Majorana bound states using 2D semi-Dirac materials. By applying Rashba spin-orbit coupling and a Zeeman field to these materials under s-wave superconductivity, the authors show that corner Majorana modes emerge at the edges of finite strips. The system can be effectively described as coupled Kitaev chains. Crucially, the study demonstrates that these corner states are remarkably robust against significant levels of Anderson disorder.
main_problem: The challenge of realizing robust, fault-tolerant Majorana bound states (MBSs) without the experimental difficulties of 1D nanowires or the need for complex engineered nanostructures and vortices.
main_result: The discovery that 2D semi-Dirac materials can host corner Majorana modes through proximity-induced superconductivity, where the system effectively acts as coupled Kitaev chains.
method: A theoretical framework using the Bogoliubov-de Gennes (BdG) formalism, including numerical diagonalization of a tight-binding model and projection onto edge-state subspaces.
model_or_system: A 2D semi-Dirac system with anisotropic bulk dispersion (quadratic in kx, linear in ky) subject to Rashba spin-orbit coupling, a perpendicular Zeeman field, and s-wave superconducting proximity effect.
key_observables: Edge-state dispersion, Majorana polarization, Majorana zero modes at corners, Local Density of States (LDOS), and energy splitting between modes.
important_parameters: Mass term (M0), Rashba coupling (alpha), Zeeman field (BZ), chemical potential (mu), and Anderson disorder strength (w).
main_assumptions: The large-width limit (W >> lambda^-1) allows treating opposite edges as decoupled; the superconducting pairing is momentum-independent; and orbital spinors of opposite edges are orthogonal.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows numerical edge-state spectra and spin/orbital components; Figure 2 compares full BdG results to a reduced decoupled model; Figure 3 displays energy spectra for semi-infinite and finite systems; Figure 6 illustrates the stability of the integrated LDOS under increasing disorder.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of Majorana realization, defines the semi-Dirac Hamiltonian and its edge-state properties, derives an effective 1D Kitaev chain model via projection, analyzes the emergence of corner modes in finite geometries, and evaluates the robustness of these modes against Anderson disorder.
why_interesting: It proposes a new, naturally occurring 2D platform for Majorana modes that avoids the complexities of engineered nanostructures, which is highly relevant for developing topological qubits for fault-tolerant quantum computing.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Electronic band structure of a semi-infinite system in y-direction (width = 90a) with translational symmetry in x-direction. The color encodes the expectation value of the relevant spin and orbital components: (a) x-component on the orbital operator, (b) y-component and (c) z-component of the spin operator, respectively. The rest of the components are nearly zero for the edge states. Panel (d) is colored according to spatial localization in y-direction. The color scales range from −1 to +1 for spin/orbital projections (from red to blue) and from -W/2 to +W/2 for ⟨y⟩(from orange to purple). The edge states remain approximately polarized in the orbital σx sector, while their spin...

Figure 2 compares the spectra obtained from the full Hamiltonian (1) in a nanoribbon geometry and the effective low-energy Hamiltonian (5). Panel (a) shows the numerical spectrum of the full Hamiltonian for a nanoribbon finite in y-direction and panel (c) displays the spectrum of the effective low-energy theory. In panel (b) we include the analytical dispersion relation of Eq. 3 for electrons (µ > 0) and holes (µ < 0) without superconducting coupling, ∆= 0, in order to highlight the good agreement between the expected gap openings according to non-zero elements of ∆eff,ξ(kx) and the numerical results. We use the color scale to show the electron (in blue) or hole (in red) character of the...

FIG. 2. Band dispersion of the semi-infinite system in the y-direction with translational symmetry in the x-direction in Nambu space. Panel (a) shows the numerical spectrum with ∆= 0.2 eV of the BdG Hamiltonian of Eq. (1) discretized in a strip with translational symmetry in the x-direction and a finite width of W = 90a in the y-direction. In panel (b) we plot the analytical energies obtained perturbatively given by Eq. (3) for µ = 0.15 eV for electrons and µ = −0.15 eV for holes, in the absence of the superconducting coupling. We use the line style to differentiate the distinct branches (ξ, S): a dotted line for (ξ, S) = (+1, +1), dotted-dashed line for (ξ, S) = (+1, −1), a dashed line for...

FIG. 3. Panels (a) and (b) display the bands of a system with translational invariance along x-direction and W = 90a. The color scale indicates the expectation value of the y-coordinate. Panels (c) and (d) display the corresponding energy spectrum of the finite-size system in both directions. Panels (a) and (c) correspond to the regime with µ < BZ, we take µ = 0.15 eV and BZ = 0.35 eV, where we find zero-energy modes inside the effective SC gap plotted with dashed lines. In contrast, panels (b) and (d) show the results for the regime without Majorana bound states, in which the low-energy coupled states belong to the same edge, i.e. µ > BZ, with µ = 0.45 eV, BZ = 0.35 eV. The rest of the...

FIG. 4. Spatially resolved Majorana polarization (MP). The arrows indicate the real and imaginary part of MP in each site. The colormap corresponds to its absolute value. In (a) we consider the four zero-energy states calculated in the maximally localized basis by diagonalizing the position operator, ˆ X + ˆY projected onto that subspace. The uniform alignment of the MP vectors indicates a well-defined Majorana phase for the corner modes. (b) shows the MP for the first state above the superconducting gap, which is an extended state along the upper edge. Unlike the zero-energy modes, the precession of the arrows indicates trivial behavior.

FIG. 5. Gap size δE as a function of the model parameters for the semi-infinite system with translational symmetry along x- direction and W = 90 Å. Dark blue indicates gap closings, plotted on a logarithmic color scale. The phase boundaries between a topological phase with Majorana zero-energy modes labeled by ZMs and a trivial one, are determined by gap closings of the edge states spectrum at kx = 0 Å. We consider the same set of parameters as in Fig. 1 varying the ones indicated in the axis.

FIG. 6. (a) Integrated LDOS of the four central states within the fixed corner mask as a function of the disorder strength w. The dashed horizontal line marks the clean-limit value, 3.88. The right panels (b-d) show the level spacing of consecutive states as a function of the disorder strength w. We extract by numerical diagonalization six states around zero energy, in the clean limit, eigenstates 0 and 5 are extended states along the edge, while 1, 2, 3, and 4 are the four corner states. The labels of the gaps follow these indexes. Accordingly, panel (b) shows ∆E45, the effective superconducting gap; panel (c) shows ∆E34, which quantifies the energy splitting between upper and lower corner...

FIG. 7. Bulk spectrum of a semi-Dirac system in terms of the sgn(M0/M1y). For M0 = 0 eV the gap is closed, distinguishing a phase transition between an insulating phase and a band inverted regime shown with the expectation value of the σz operator.

FIG. 8. Electronic band structure of a semi-infinite system in y-direction (width = 90a) with translational symmetry in x- direction. The colormap shows the expectation value of the orbital part (a)-(c) and panel (d) shows the expectation value of the y-coordinate. The color scales range from −1 to +1 (from red to blue) and from -W/2 to +W/2 for spatial location (from orange to purple). The edge states are essentially polarized in the x component, whose value is associated with the edge on which they live. This figure has been made using parameters normalized to the bulk gap M0, µ = 0.0 eV, M0 = 1.0 eV, M1x = 2.3 eV · Å2, M1y = 2.3 eV · Å2, Vx = −3.8 eV · Å2, Vy = −5.0 eV · Å, α = 0.0 Å−1,...
